old_claim.note: Bronze Casting Molds provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Series production of cheap cast badges depended on metal casting and molds; the broader metal-casting-mold node fits better than bronze-specific molds.

source_shape.source_locator: The Kunera overview says cheap castwork was crucial for spreading late-medieval badge imagery and notes that examples from the same casting mold are rarely recovered despite series production.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Pilgrim badges and ampullae were produced as cheap cast metal series, not as bronze objects requiring a bronze-specific mold technology.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge should point to general metal casting molds because the evidence stresses cast production and molds while the object material is tin-lead or lead alloy, not bronze.

ontology_before: Pilgrim Badge Casting depended on bronze casting molds, implying a bronze-specific object or mold technology for badges normally made as cheap tin-lead or lead-alloy castings.
ontology_after: Pilgrim Badge Casting depends on metal_casting_molds for the cast-series production mechanism, while bronze-specific mold technology remains outside the scoped node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: pilgrim_badge_casting now depends on metal_casting_molds and no longer directly depends on bronze_casting_molds.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to bronze badge molds rather than tin-lead or lead-alloy cast badges.
A source shows the scoped mass-produced pilgrim badges were normally die-stamped rather than cast.
A dedicated badge_mold_making node is added and should replace broad metal_casting_molds.
short_reason: Metal casting molds match cast lead-alloy badge production better than bronze-specific molds.
